attributes.exploration target: Jovian rings and inner moons area
attributes.planned flybys: moons Thebe, Amalthea, Adrastea, and Metis
attributes.mission goal: measure Jupiter's composition, gravitational field, magnetic field, and polar magnetosphere
attributes.search objective: clues about how the planet formed, including whether it has a rocky core, the amount of water present within the deep atmosphere, mass distribution, and its deep winds
attributes.mission_type: European-led mission to the Jupiter system
attributes.fuel_required_for_encounter: about 35% of New Horizons' available trajectory-adjustment fuel supply
attributes.definition: object passes in front of a star from the vantage point of Earth
attributes.location: Applied Physics Laboratory in Howard County, Maryland
attributes.event_cause: over-assignment of commanded science operations on the craft's approach to Pluto
attributes.impact: without major impacts on its mission
attributes.mitigation: reduced the number of scientific operations on the craft
attributes.chilling_method: passive radiator on the antisolar face of the spacecraft
attributes.dynamic_range: full dynamic range of brightness information
attributes.primary_role: determine the relative concentrations of various elements and isotopes in Pluto's atmosphere
attributes.purpose: count the electrons, protons and helium ions, and measure their properties such as velocity, density, and temperature
attributes.TCM_1A: first trajectory-correction maneuver (TCM), which was divided into two parts (TCM-1A and TCM-1B)
attributes.TCM_1B: first trajectory-correction maneuver (TCM), which was divided into two parts (TCM-1A and TCM-1B)
attributes.observations: phase curve and a search for small moons
attributes.name: J. G.
attributes.upper stage: based on the Star 48BV solid rocket motor
attributes.understanding_goal: understand the particles' lifecycles — where they came from, how they became accelerated and how they move out from the Sun through interplanetary space
attributes.EPI_Lo_measurement: measures the spectra of electrons and ions and identifies carbon, oxygen, neon, magnesium, silicon, iron and two isotopes of helium, He-3 and He-4
attributes.EPI_Hi_measurement: uses three particle sensors composed of stacked layers of detectors to measure particles with energies higher than those measured by EPI-Lo
attributes.EPI_Hi_front_layers: composed of ultra-thin silicon detectors made up of geometric segments
attributes.EPI_Hi_measurement Capability: detect up to 100,000 particles per second
attributes.EPI_Hi_identification_method: measuring how deep they travel into the stack of detectors and how many electrons they pull off atoms in each detector
attributes.Principal Investigator: Justin Kasper at the University of Michigan and the Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory
attributes.camera_detectors: radiation-hardened Active Pixel Sensor CMOS detectors
attributes.lens_material_commonality: common type of glass used for space telescopes
attributes.lens_material_hardening: sufficiently hardened against the impacts of dust
attributes.main_instruments: Solar Probe Analyzers (SPAN, two electrostatic analyzers) and the Solar Probe Cup (SPC)
attributes.SPC_orientation: Peeking over the heat shield to measure how electrons and ions are moving
attributes.SPC_environment: exposed to the full light, heat and energy of the Sun
attributes.SPC_composition: series of highly transparent grids — one of which uses variable high voltages to sort the particles — above several collector plates, which measure the particles' properties
attributes.SPC_grid_function: sort the particles and help sort out background noise
attributes.SPAN_field_of_view: wide fields of view to allow them to see the parts of space not observed by SPC
attributes.SPAN_sorting_method: maze that sends the particles through a series of deflectors and voltages to sort the particles based on their mass and charge
attributes.SPAN_A_components: two components to measure both electrons and ions

attributes.formation_time: early in the Solar System's evolution
attributes.name: (181708) 1993 FW
attributes.results_presented: at the 50th meeting of the American Astronomical Society Division for Planetary Sciences
attributes.position: outer boundary of the Oort cloud
attributes.canceled_by: Sean O'Keefe as Administrator of NASA
attributes.canceled_as_part_of: President George W. Bush's request for the 2003 United States federal budget
attributes.canceled_reason: need for a restructuring of NASA and its projects, falling in line with the Bush Administration's wish for NASA to refocus on 'research and development, and addressing management shortcomings'
attributes.capability during perihelion: fly from New York to Tokyo in just over a minute
attributes.purpose: captures the scale and shape of electric and magnetic fields in the Sun's atmosphere
attributes.measurement_target: properties of the fast and slow solar wind
attributes.measurement_process: understand the fields associated with waves, shocks and magnetic reconnection
attributes.antenna_orientation: four stick out beyond the spacecraft's heat shield and into the sunlight, one sticks out perpendicular to the others in the shade of the heat shield
attributes.magnetometer_type: search coil magnetometer (SCM) and two fluxgate magnetometers (MAGi and MAGo)
attributes.SCM_measurement: measures how the magnetic field changes over time
attributes.MAGi/MAGo_measurement: measure the large-scale coronal magnetic field
attributes.MAGi/MAGo_specialization: measuring the magnetic field further from the Sun where it varies at a slower rate
attributes.SCM_specialization: necessary closer to the Sun where the field changes quickly
attributes.Principal Investigator: Stuart Bale at the University of California, Berkeley

attributes.journal: Quarterly Journal of the Royal Astronomical Society

attributes.action: announced Arrokoth as a potential target of the New Horizons probe

relationships.target: Arrokoth as a potential target of the New Horizons probe

attributes.name: stars
attributes.Science_Phase_definition: portion within 0.25 AU of each orbit around the Sun
attributes.Science_Phase_during_perihelion: runs for a few days both before and after each perihelion
attributes.speed_increase: as much as 300,000 miles per hour (480,000 km/h)

attributes.antennas: high-gain antenna, two backup low-gain antennas, and a medium-gain dish
attributes.scientific instrument data: recorded to its solid-state memory buffer at each encounter
attributes.solid-state memory buffer: two low-power solid-state recorders (one primary, one backup)
attributes.radio signals: took four hours and 25 minutes to traverse 4.7 billion km of space
attributes.launch_site: Pad 41 at Cape Canaveral Air Force Station, Florida
attributes.deceleration: then slow down again afterward until it reaches its aphelion
attributes.beacon_transmission: received on December 26 confirming successful passage
attributes.thrusters: will eventually run out of fuel
attributes.instruments: will be exposed to the full radiance of the Sun for the first time
attributes.housing: housed together in IEMs (integrated electronics modules)
attributes.purpose: keeping all parts of a spacecraft within acceptable temperature ranges

attributes.effect: provided further deceleration relative to its heliocentric orbit

attributes.planned_as: first three missions of the OPSP program

attributes.event_consequence: rebooted itself, causing the spacecraft to go into safe mode
attributes.impact: some data loss on Jupiter's magnetotail

attributes.visit_status: not visited since the Voyager 2 flybys in the 1980s

attributes.aperture: largest aperture of any such instrument ever flown

attributes.close encounters with the Sun: first of three final. closest encounters will take place on Dec. 24. 2024
attributes.purpose: make observations of the Sun's outer corona
attributes.velocity comparison: almost three times as fast as the previous record holder, Helios-2
attributes.deployed_antennas: high-gain antenna, magnetometer boom, and electric field antennas
attributes.first_Venus_flyby_purpose: reduce the probe's speed and orbit closer to the Sun
attributes.Science_Phase_definition: portion within 0.25 AU of each orbit around the Sun
attributes.Science_Phase_during_perihelion: runs for a few days both before and after each perihelion
attributes.transmission_during_non_Science_Phase: transmitting data from the science phase
attributes.communication_overshadowing_cause_1: heat shield between the antenna and Earth
attributes.communication_overshadowing_cause_2: angle between the probe and the Sun, as seen from Earth, is too small
attributes.third_Venus_flyby_encounter_time: less than two orbits, 198 days later
attributes.third_elliptical_orbit_period: half of that of Venus, or about 112.5 days
attributes.fifth_elliptical_orbit_period: about 96 days, three-sevenths that of Venus
attributes.sixth_Venus_flyby_encounter_time: almost two years after the fifth
attributes.seventh_Venus_flyby_encounter_time: After five more orbits (two orbits of Venus)
attributes.previous_names: Solar Probe, Solar Probe Plus, Solar Probe+
attributes.closest_approach_distance: 9.86 solar radii (6.9 million km or 4.3 million miles)
attributes.designer: Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory
attributes.honoree_title: professor emeritus at the University of Chicago
attributes.closest_approach_record: closest ever artificial object to the Sun
attributes.previous_record_distance: 42.73 million kilometers (26.55 million miles) from the Sun's surface
attributes.final_closest_approach_distance: 6.1 million km (3.8 million miles) from the surface
attributes.proposed_by: Fields and Particles Group, Committee 8 of the National Academy of Sciences' Space Science Board
attributes.proposed_mission: solar probe to pass inside the orbit of Mercury to study the particles and fields in the vicinity of the Sun
attributes.memory_card_content: copy of his 1958 scientific paper predicting important aspects of solar physics
attributes.spacecraft trajectory: gradually shrink its elliptical orbit around the Sun
attributes.role: providing theoretical input and independent assessment of scientific performance to the Science Working Group (SWG) and the SPP Project to maximize the scientific return from the mission
attributes.Principal Investigator: Marco Velli at the University of California, Los Angeles and the Jet Propulsion Laboratory

attributes.role: professor emeritus at the University of Chicago
attributes.contribution: developed a mathematical theory that predicted the existence of solar wind

attributes.awardees: teams from NASA, Johns Hopkins, and partners
